q: How do you use the Databricks Marketplace and Model Hub to find and deploy a fine-tuned embedding model for a specialized domain RAG pipeline?
a: Databricks Marketplace is accessible from the Data & AI menu in the workspace. To find an embedding model, filter by product type 'Models' and category 'Embeddings'. Each listing shows the model card, license terms, benchmark scores, and supported languages. Once you select a model, clicking 'Get instant access' provisions the model into your workspace's Unity Catalog under the provider's catalog namespace. From there you deploy it as a Model Serving endpoint: go to Serving, click Create, select the UC-registered model, choose CPU or GPU compute, and click Create Endpoint. For a specialized domain (e.g., biomedical or legal), a fine-tuned embedding model like `bge-large-en` fine-tuned on domain corpora will produce higher retrieval precision than general-purpose models because the embedding space aligns with domain vocabulary. After deployment, reference the endpoint name when creating an AI Search Delta Sync index via `embedding_model_endpoint_name`. Track the model version and license in UC model tags so your RAG pipeline has full lineage from marketplace listing to production serving.
fj: Always use the default `databricks-bge-large-en` embedding model because Databricks optimizes it for all use cases.
fs: A general-purpose embedding model is a reasonable default but is not optimal for every domain. Biomedical, legal, and code domains benefit from domain-adapted embeddings where the token representations reflect domain-specific semantic relationships. Marketplace provides domain-fine-tuned alternatives that can meaningfully improve retrieval precision.

q: What is the Databricks Mosaic AI Gateway (Unity AI Gateway), and how does it differ from simply calling a Foundation Model API endpoint directly?
a: Unity AI Gateway is a governance and control layer that sits between callers and Foundation Model API (or external model) endpoints. Calling an endpoint directly bypasses all policy controls — anyone with the endpoint URL and a valid token can call it without rate limits, guardrails, or usage attribution. Unity AI Gateway adds: (1) identity-based access control — only users/groups explicitly permitted can invoke the endpoint; (2) rate limiting — per-user or per-group token/request caps that return 429 when exceeded; (3) input and output guardrails — PII detection/redaction, toxicity filtering, and prompt injection detection enforced at the gateway layer before the request reaches the model; (4) inference table logging — request/response payload capture to a Unity Catalog Delta table; (5) usage table logging — aggregated cost and token data in system tables for financial attribution. The gateway is configured as properties on the Model Serving endpoint, not as a separate service. This means all calls to the endpoint automatically go through the gateway — there is no way to bypass it for users without direct serving infrastructure access. This unified policy plane is the reason Databricks calls it a 'single control plane for your AI estate'.
fj: Unity AI Gateway is a separate standalone service you deploy on Kubernetes alongside your Model Serving endpoint.
fs: Unity AI Gateway is not a separate service — it is a configuration layer on Databricks Model Serving endpoints, natively integrated into the platform. There is no Kubernetes deployment involved. This embedded architecture means governance applies automatically to all callers without requiring infrastructure changes.

q: Your RAG pipeline retrieves chunks from a Unity Catalog Delta table that contains rows with `status = 'draft'` as well as `status = 'approved'`. How do you ensure only approved documents are returned in production queries?
a: Apply a row-level filter function to the source Delta table that restricts reads to `status = 'approved'` rows. Create a SQL masking or filter function in Unity Catalog, attach it to the table with `ALTER TABLE ... ADD ROW FILTER`, and the AI Search Delta Sync index will inherit this filter because it reads through the Unity Catalog connector. This means the index will only embed and store approved chunks. For the serving endpoint's service principal, additionally ensure it has only SELECT privileges — not INSERT or UPDATE — so it cannot modify document status. For real-time query filtering, you can also pass a metadata filter to the AI Search similarity search call: `filters={'status': 'approved'}`. The Delta Sync approach is stronger because it prevents draft content from ever entering the vector index, while the query-time filter approach still indexes draft content (a security risk if the filter is ever accidentally omitted). Combining both — row filter on the source table AND a query-time filter as defense-in-depth — is the recommended production pattern.
fj: Filter the results in Python after the AI Search query returns them by checking `doc.metadata['status'] == 'approved'`.
fs: Post-retrieval filtering in Python means draft documents were retrieved from the vector index and potentially processed by the LLM before being dropped. This wastes tokens, leaks existence of draft content, and is fragile if the filter check is accidentally removed. Unity Catalog row-level filters prevent draft content from being indexed or retrieved in the first place.

code.label: Query-time metadata filter on AI Search

q: How do you define an MLflow model signature for a chat-style RAG endpoint, and why is it required for deployment?
a: An MLflow model signature declares the expected input and output schema as a contract between the model artifact and the serving infrastructure. For a chat-style RAG endpoint, the input schema follows the OpenAI messages format: a column named `messages` typed as an array of objects, each with `role` (string) and `content` (string) fields. The output schema declares `answer` (string) and `sources` (array of strings). You define the signature using `mlflow.models.ModelSignature` with `Schema` and `ColSpec` (or `Array` / `Object` property types from `mlflow.types.schema`). Alternatively, if you provide an `input_example` when calling `log_model()`, MLflow infers the signature automatically by running the model on the example. Deployment to Model Serving requires a signature: the endpoint's request validation layer uses it to reject malformed payloads before they reach your model, returning a 400 error with a clear schema mismatch message instead of an opaque runtime exception. The signature is also displayed in the Unity Catalog model page for discoverability.
fj: Skip the signature — MLflow will infer it automatically from the first real request after deployment.
fs: MLflow does not infer signatures at serving time from live traffic. Signature inference only happens at log_model() time when an input_example is provided, or when you call mlflow.infer_signature() explicitly. Deploying without a signature means the endpoint has no input validation, and callers get no contract documentation in Unity Catalog.

code.label: Define chat model signature

q: Describe the Databricks AI Search Delta Sync index pipeline modes — TRIGGERED vs CONTINUOUS — and when to choose each.
a: A Delta Sync index keeps its vectors up to date by reading changes from the source Delta table. TRIGGERED mode requires explicit sync calls: you invoke `index.sync()` programmatically — for example, at the end of a Lakeflow Declarative Pipeline run or on a Databricks Workflow schedule. The sync reads the Delta change data feed (CDF) for rows added, updated, or deleted since the last sync and updates the index accordingly. TRIGGERED is appropriate when data changes in batches (nightly ETL loads, weekly document refreshes) and real-time freshness is not required. CONTINUOUS mode monitors the source Delta table and applies changes within seconds, operating like a micro-batch stream. It is appropriate for use cases where users must see the latest documents immediately — customer support with live knowledge base updates, or security alerts that must be searchable as soon as they are written. CONTINUOUS mode requires that Delta Change Data Feed is enabled on the source table (`ALTER TABLE ... SET TBLPROPERTIES (delta.enableChangeDataFeed = true)`). Storage-Optimized endpoints only support TRIGGERED mode; Standard endpoints support both.
fj: Use CONTINUOUS mode for all indexes because fresher data is always better.
fs: CONTINUOUS mode carries a higher operational cost due to near-real-time streaming overhead. For workloads where data changes infrequently (e.g., a policy document repository updated weekly), TRIGGERED mode is more cost-efficient and simpler to operate. Over-engineering freshness adds cost without user benefit.

q: How does the Mosaic AI Agent Framework handle tool-calling for agents on Databricks, and what types of tools can be registered?
a: In the Mosaic AI Agent Framework, tools are the mechanism by which agents take actions beyond text generation. A tool is any callable function exposed to the LLM; the LLM decides at inference time which tool to call based on its instructions and the user query. Tool types on Databricks: (1) Unity Catalog (UC) Functions — Python or SQL functions registered in Unity Catalog under a three-level name; they are the preferred tool type because they inherit UC governance (RBAC, audit, lineage); (2) MLflow-logged Python functions — arbitrary Python callables packaged as MLflow PyFunc models; (3) Genie Space endpoints — expose a Genie Space's NL2SQL capability as a tool; (4) External APIs — called via HTTP using UC connections that store credentials securely; (5) MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers — third-party tool integrations via the emerging MCP standard. Each tool is defined with a name, description, and input/output schema; the description is what the LLM reads to decide whether to invoke the tool. Well-written tool descriptions are critical — vague descriptions cause the LLM to invoke the wrong tool. MLflow Tracing records every tool invocation as a child span, including input arguments and return values, enabling full auditability.
fj: Hard-code all business logic inside the LLM's system prompt using detailed instructions, so no external tool calls are needed.
fs: Hard-coded system prompt logic cannot call APIs, query databases, or retrieve live data. Tools extend the agent's capabilities beyond token prediction — they enable real-world actions like running SQL, calling search APIs, and writing to databases. Without tools, an agent is limited to knowledge encoded in its weights, which is stale and unverifiable.

RETURNS TABLE (chunk_id STRING, text STRING, score DOUBLE)
COMMENT 'Search the product knowledge base for relevant chunks. Use for any product-related question.'

q: What is the purpose of MLflow Tracing in the Mosaic AI Agent Framework, and what information does a trace capture?
a: MLflow Tracing is the observability backbone of the Mosaic AI Agent Framework. When enabled, it automatically wraps every significant operation in the agent's execution into a span and assembles spans into a hierarchical trace tree. A trace captures: the root span (the full agent invocation) with total latency, input payload, and output; child spans for each LLM call (prompt tokens, completion tokens, model name, latency); tool invocation spans (tool name, input arguments, return value, latency); retriever spans (query text, number of results, returned chunks with scores); and any error spans if an exception occurred. Traces are stored in the MLflow Tracking server and surfaced in the Databricks UI under the Traces tab of an MLflow experiment. In production, every request to a Model Serving endpoint with tracing enabled emits a trace that feeds Agent Monitoring dashboards. Traces enable debugging of multi-step agent failures — you can see exactly which LLM call produced a hallucination, which tool returned an unexpected result, or where latency was spent. Custom spans can be added with `@mlflow.trace` decorator or `mlflow.start_span()` context manager for application-specific operations.
fj: Add Python `logging` statements throughout the agent code to track execution flow.
fs: Python logging produces flat text output that is hard to correlate across concurrent requests and does not capture structured data like token counts, tool arguments, or span latencies. MLflow Tracing produces a structured, queryable, hierarchical record per request that integrates with evaluation and monitoring workflows.

code.label: Add custom span to an agent step

scored = [(chunk, cross_encoder_score(query, chunk)) for chunk in chunks]
return [c for c, _ in sorted(scored, key=lambda x: -x[1])[:3]]

q: How do you handle the case where a Databricks AI Search index needs to reflect deleted source documents (e.g., when a compliance document is revoked)?
a: Databricks AI Search Delta Sync indexes support deletion via the Delta Change Data Feed (CDF). When a row is deleted from the source Delta table (using `DELETE FROM catalog.schema.chunks WHERE doc_id = 'revoked_doc'`), the change is recorded in the Delta CDF log. On the next TRIGGERED sync (or automatically for CONTINUOUS mode), the AI Search index reads the deleted row IDs and removes the corresponding embedding vectors from the index. Prerequisites: (1) CDF must be enabled on the source table: `ALTER TABLE catalog.schema.chunks SET TBLPROPERTIES (delta.enableChangeDataFeed = true)`; (2) the index was created with `pipeline_type='TRIGGERED'` or `'CONTINUOUS'` (not `DIRECT_ACCESS`). After a sync, querying for the deleted document's content should return zero results. For compliance scenarios requiring immediate removal (e.g., GDPR right-to-erasure), use CONTINUOUS mode or trigger a manual sync immediately after the DELETE. Hard-delete from the Delta table (as opposed to soft-deleting via a `status` flag) is required for the vector to actually be removed from the index; a status flag change only prevents retrieval if a query-time filter is also in place.
fj: Recreate the entire AI Search index from scratch whenever a document needs to be removed.
fs: Full index recreation is expensive and introduces downtime or stale results during the rebuild. Delta CDF-based incremental sync handles deletions efficiently by only processing the changed rows. Full rebuilds should only be needed for index schema changes or endpoint type migrations.

q: A team wants to use `ai_query()` in a Lakeflow Declarative Pipeline to enrich a Silver table with LLM-generated sentiment labels. Walk through the implementation.
a: Integrating `ai_query()` into a Lakeflow Declarative Pipeline (formerly DLT) enables fully automated, governed LLM enrichment within a streaming or batch data pipeline. In the pipeline notebook, define the enriched table using the `@dlt.table` decorator. Inside the function, read from the upstream Silver table and apply `ai_query()` as a SQL expression via `spark.sql()` or directly in a `selectExpr()` call. The pipeline handles retry logic, checkpointing, and lineage automatically. Important: use Databricks-hosted Foundation Model API endpoints (prefixed with `databricks-`) for `ai_query()` batch calls — provisioned-throughput endpoints are not compatible. Set the pipeline cluster to Databricks Runtime 15.4 LTS or later. Cost is recorded under MODEL_SERVING / BATCH_INFERENCE in system tables. For incremental processing, use STREAMING mode so only new or changed rows are sent to the LLM, preventing redundant inference on previously processed records.
fj: Write a Spark Python UDF that calls the Foundation Model API endpoint directly inside the Lakeflow pipeline.
fs: A manual Python UDF calling the REST API lacks the automatic retry, parallelization, and rate-limit handling that `ai_query()` provides natively. `ai_query()` also produces richer cost telemetry in system tables. While a custom UDF works, `ai_query()` is the official, supported, and more observable pattern for SQL-native batch inference.

q: How do you version and compare prompt templates across experiments using the MLflow Prompt Registry on Databricks?
a: The MLflow Prompt Registry enables Git-like versioning for prompt templates with experiment linkage. Each call to `mlflow.create_prompt()` or `mlflow.update_prompt()` creates a new immutable version with an auto-incrementing number and an optional commit message. To compare prompt versions, link each version to an MLflow experiment run: when you run an evaluation with prompt version 2, log the prompt URI (`prompts:/catalog.genai.rag_prompt/2`) as a run parameter using `mlflow.log_param('prompt_version', 'prompts:/catalog.genai.rag_prompt/2')`. This creates traceable lineage from prompt version to evaluation metrics. In the MLflow Experiment Compare view, add `prompt_version` as a column alongside quality metrics to see directly which prompt version achieved the best groundedness or answer correctness scores. Aliases (`production`, `staging`) act as mutable pointers — updating the `production` alias to point to version 4 requires no redeployment because the serving code loads the prompt at runtime via `mlflow.load_prompt('prompts:/catalog.genai.rag_prompt@production')`. Non-technical prompt engineers can iterate via the Databricks UI prompt editor, creating new versions without code changes.
fj: Keep prompt versions in separate Python files (prompt_v1.py, prompt_v2.py) and comment out old versions.
fs: File-based version management in source code conflates prompt lifecycle with code deployment cycles. It prevents non-engineers from iterating on prompts, makes rollback slow (requires a code deployment), and produces no structured lineage between prompt versions and evaluation results. The Prompt Registry is purpose-built for this use case.

q: How do you chunk source documents optimally for a Databricks AI Search RAG pipeline, and what chunking parameters matter most?
a: Chunking strategy directly affects retrieval precision: chunks that are too large dilute relevance; chunks that are too small lose context. The most important parameters are chunk size and chunk overlap. Chunk size (measured in tokens, not characters) should match the embedding model's effective context window — for `bge-large-en`, 256–512 tokens is standard for paragraph-level retrieval. Chunk overlap (typically 10–20% of chunk size, e.g., 50 tokens for a 512-token chunk) ensures that sentences near chunk boundaries appear in at least one chunk fully, preventing boundary artifacts where a key sentence is split across two chunks and captured in neither. The splitter type also matters: `RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter` from LangChain tries to split on paragraph breaks, then sentences, then words — preserving semantic units. For structured documents (HTML, Markdown), use element-aware splitters that respect heading hierarchy. For code files, use language-aware splitters. After chunking, each chunk row in the Delta table should retain source metadata (file path, page number, section heading) as separate columns so retrieval results carry provenance. Experiment with two or three chunking strategies on a small evaluation set and compare retrieval metrics using MLflow before committing to production.
fj: Use a fixed character length of 500 characters with no overlap for all document types because it is simple and consistent.
fs: Character-based splits ignore token boundaries, causing unpredictable tokenization behavior. A 500-character chunk can be 80 tokens or 200 tokens depending on word length, leading to inconsistent embedding quality. Zero overlap means that content spanning two adjacent chunks may never appear in a single retrievable unit. Token-aware splitting with overlap is the correct approach.

code.label: Chunk documents with RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
